## Conclusion — What the Map Is For

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The real conclusion (folds the old "what this map is good for"). Four beats — what it shows, the diagnostic, the honest caveat, the call to action. No new content; this is a landing.
:::

The map is useful for one specific thing: locating where a disagreement actually lives. If you come away seeing that Christiano and Yudkowsky don't disagree about how bad a misaligned superintelligence would be — both think: very bad — but about whether today's models are evidence and whether warning shots are actionable, the map worked. If you can see that Bengio and Yudkowsky share a threat model almost entirely and split on a single crux — whether agency is emergent or chosen — the map worked.

And it gives you an order of operations. When a debate stalls, don't trade p(doom) numbers. First ask whether you share a **frame** — the same definition of doom, the same time window, the same standard for what counts as evidence. If you don't, that is the disagreement, and no amount of evidence will close it; what looks like an argument about the danger is really two people running different questions. Only once the frame is shared is it worth finding the **crux** — the factual bet you actually differ on — because that is the disagreement evidence can move.

An honest caveat: the map takes a position by existing. It assumes the debate is in good faith, that decomposition is the productive move, and that these disagreements are legible to this kind of analysis — a decomposition-first, empirically-conditioned stance that a committed prior-dominant thinker may find beside the point. If you've read this far and feel it missed something essential about the structure of the problem, that, too, is worth naming — and it will be a disagreement about the frame, not the facts.

So: the next time you are stuck, stop asking for a number. Ask first whether you are even answering the same question — and then ask which crux the other person is treating as settled.
